amazon-athena.text:
• Serverless interactive query service — no infrastructure to manage
• Query data directly in Amazon S3 using standard SQL
• Supports CSV, JSON, ORC, Avro, Parquet formats
• Pay per query: $5 per TB of data scanned
• Use columnar formats (Parquet, ORC) to reduce costs and improve performance
• Built on Presto engine

• One-time SQL queries on S3 objects
• Serverless log analysis (CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, ELB access logs)
• IoT data analytics stored in S3
• Business intelligence and ad-hoc analysis
• Query VPC Flow Logs or CloudTrail logs directly — no ETL needed
• Commonly paired with AWS Glue Data Catalog as the metadata store

• Use columnar data formats (Parquet, ORC) — reduces amount of data scanned
• Compress data (gzip, snappy) to lower scan size and cost
• Partition datasets in S3 (e.g., by date/region) to limit scanned data
• Use larger files (>128 MB) to avoid small-file overhead
• Federated Query: query data in sources outside S3 (RDS, DynamoDB, on-prem) using Lambda data source connectors

aws-data-exchange.text:
• Service to find, subscribe to, and use third-party data in the cloud
• Data providers publish datasets; subscribers access them directly in S3
• Supports data files, APIs, Amazon Redshift, and Amazon S3 datasets
• Eliminates manual data procurement, licensing, and delivery
• Integrated with AWS Marketplace for billing

• Data products are published by providers and versioned as revisions
• Subscribers receive new data automatically when providers publish revisions
• Data can be exported to S3 and used with Athena, Redshift, or EMR
• Useful for financial data, geospatial data, weather data, healthcare datasets
• No need to build/maintain data ingestion pipelines for third-party data

amazon-data-firehose.text:
• Fully managed service for loading streaming data into data stores and analytics tools
• Near real-time delivery (buffers data before delivering — NOT real-time like Kinesis Data Streams)
• No administration required — fully serverless and auto-scales
• Supported destinations: Amazon S3, Amazon Redshift, Amazon OpenSearch Service, Splunk, Datadog, HTTP endpoints, MongoDB Cloud, New Relic, Dynatrace

• Can transform data in-flight using AWS Lambda before delivery
• Buffer size (1–128 MB) and buffer interval (60–900 seconds) control delivery frequency
• Supports data compression (gzip, snappy, zip) and encryption before S3 storage
• Key exam distinction: Firehose = near real-time / managed delivery; Kinesis Data Streams = real-time / custom consumers
• Cannot write to DynamoDB directly as a destination
• Ideal pattern: IoT → Kinesis Data Streams → Firehose → S3 → Athena

amazon-emr.text:
• Elastic MapReduce — managed cluster platform for big data frameworks
• Supports Apache Hadoop, Spark, HBase, Presto, Flink, and more
• Runs on EC2 instances (or EKS / Outposts)
• Used for data processing, machine learning, and interactive analytics at petabyte scale
• Data stored in HDFS (ephemeral) or EMRFS (persisted to S3)

amazon-emr.part: Amazon EMR: Node Types & Use Cases (SAA-C03)
amazon-emr.text:
• Master node: manages the cluster and coordinates tasks
• Core node: runs tasks and stores data in HDFS
• Task node: runs tasks only — no data storage, can be Spot Instances
• Use Spot Instances for task nodes to reduce cost
• Use case: large-scale ETL, log analysis, machine learning, genomics
• Long-running cluster vs. transient cluster (spun up per job, then terminated)

aws-glue.text:
• Fully managed serverless ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) service
• Automatically discovers and catalogs data schema via Glue Crawlers
• Glue Data Catalog: central metadata repository — works with Athena, Redshift Spectrum, EMR
• Generates ETL code in Python or Scala automatically
• Glue Job Bookmarks: tracks previously processed data to avoid reprocessing

• Glue Crawler: scans data in S3, RDS, DynamoDB, JDBC and populates the Data Catalog
• Glue ETL Jobs: transform and move data between sources and targets
• Glue DataBrew: visual no-code data preparation tool
• Glue Elastic Views: combine and replicate data across multiple data stores using SQL
• Streaming ETL: process real-time data from Kinesis or Kafka
• Common pattern: S3 raw data → Glue Crawler → Data Catalog → Athena queries

amazon-kinesis.text:
• Platform for real-time streaming data collection, processing, and analysis
• Family of services: Kinesis Data Streams, Kinesis Data Firehose, Kinesis Data Analytics, Kinesis Video Streams
• Use case: clickstream, IoT telemetry, application logs, financial transactions in real time

• Real-time data streaming with millisecond latency
• Data retained from 1 day (default) up to 365 days
• Composed of Shards: 1 MB/s write, 2 MB/s read per shard
• Producers: SDK, Kinesis Producer Library (KPL), Kinesis Agent
• Consumers: SDK, Kinesis Client Library (KCL), Lambda, Firehose, Analytics
• Capacity modes: Provisioned (pay per shard) or On-demand (auto-scale)
• Exam key: KDS = real-time; Firehose = near real-time (buffered)

• Run SQL queries or Apache Flink apps on real-time streaming data
• Input sources: Kinesis Data Streams or Kinesis Data Firehose
• Output to: Kinesis Data Streams, Firehose, or Lambda
• Use case: real-time dashboards, anomaly detection, time-series analytics
• Fully managed and auto-scales — no servers to manage

aws-lake-formation.text:
• Service to build, secure, and manage a data lake in days instead of months
• Built on top of Amazon S3 and AWS Glue
• Centralizes data access control using fine-grained permissions (column, row, cell level)
• Integrates with Athena, Redshift Spectrum, EMR, and Glue

• Cross-account data sharing: share Data Catalog resources with other AWS accounts
• Blueprint templates: automate ingestion from databases and log files into the lake
• Fine-grained access control: grant permissions at database, table, column, or row level
• Tag-based access control (LF-Tags): simplify permissions at scale
• Replaces complex S3 bucket policies + IAM combos for data lake governance
• Exam tip: use Lake Formation when you need centralized, fine-grained access control over a data lake

amazon-managed-streaming-for-apache-kafk.text:
• Fully managed Apache Kafka service — no need to manage brokers or ZooKeeper
• Deploy Kafka clusters in your VPC across multiple AZs
• Supports MSK Connect: deploy Kafka Connect workers to move data between Kafka and other systems
• Supports MSK Serverless: run Kafka without managing capacity

• Producers → Kafka Topics (stored in brokers) → Consumers
• Data persisted to EBS volumes within the cluster (customizable retention)
• MSK vs Kinesis: MSK supports larger message sizes (default 1 MB, configurable up to 10 MB); Kinesis max is 1 MB
• MSK integrates with Lambda, Glue Streaming ETL, Kinesis Data Analytics (Flink), S3
• Use case: real-time pipelines, event-driven microservices, streaming analytics
• High availability: multi-AZ deployment with automatic failover

amazon-opensearch-service.text:
• Managed service based on open-source OpenSearch (successor to Amazon Elasticsearch Service)
• Used for search, log analytics, real-time application monitoring, and clickstream analytics
• Automatically provisions, patches, backs up, and monitors clusters
• Supports OpenSearch Dashboards (formerly Kibana) for visualization

• Ingestion: CloudWatch Logs, Kinesis Data Firehose, Logstash, or custom apps
• Common pattern: DynamoDB Streams → Lambda → OpenSearch (for search on DynamoDB data)
• Common pattern: CloudWatch Logs → Subscription Filter → Lambda → OpenSearch
• Multi-AZ deployment for high availability
• Index State Management (ISM): automate index rollover and deletion policies
• Exam tip: OpenSearch is the go-to for full-text search and log/event analytics on AWS

amazon-quicksuite.text:
• Serverless cloud-native BI (Business Intelligence) service
• Create interactive dashboards and visualizations from multiple data sources
• Pay-per-session pricing model — cost-effective for large user bases
• SPICE: Super-fast, Parallel, In-memory Calculation Engine — caches data for faster queries

• Data sources: S3, Athena, RDS, Aurora, Redshift, DynamoDB, on-premises databases, SaaS apps
• ML Insights: anomaly detection, forecasting, and narrative auto-generation built in
• Row-level security (RLS): control which rows users can see in datasets
• Column-level security (CLS): control which columns users can access
• Use case: KPI dashboards, ad-hoc analytics, business reporting
• Exam tip: QuickSight = BI/visualization layer; does NOT store data — uses sources like Athena + S3

amazon-redshift.text:
• Fully managed petabyte-scale data warehouse service
• Columnar storage for analytical (OLAP) workloads — optimized for aggregations
• Based on PostgreSQL — NOT used for OLTP workloads
• Nodes: Leader node (query planning) + Compute nodes (data storage + processing)
• Cluster types: Single-node or Multi-node

• Redshift Spectrum: query data directly in S3 without loading it into Redshift
• Enhanced VPC Routing: forces COPY/UNLOAD traffic through your VPC
• Snapshots: automated (every 8 hours or 5 GB) or manual — stored in S3, cross-region copy supported
• Redshift Serverless: run analytics workloads without provisioning a cluster
• Redshift ML: create, train, and deploy ML models using SQL
• Concurrency Scaling: add read capacity automatically during spikes
• AQUA (Advanced Query Accelerator): hardware-accelerated cache for faster queries

amazon-eventbridge.text:
• Serverless event bus for building event-driven applications
• Successor to CloudWatch Events — same underlying API
• Three event bus types: Default (AWS services), Custom (your apps), Partner (SaaS integrations)
• Rules: match incoming events and route them to one or more targets

• Integrates with 90+ AWS services as event sources automatically
• Only AWS service that integrates directly with SaaS partners (Zendesk, Datadog, etc.)
• Schema Registry: automatically discovers and stores event schemas
• Event Replay: archive and replay past events for debugging or reprocessing
• Pipes: point-to-point integrations between a source and target with optional filtering/enrichment
• Targets: Lambda, SNS, SQS, Kinesis, Step Functions, API Gateway, and more
• Exam tip: prefer EventBridge over SNS for SaaS integrations and event-driven decoupling

amazon-simple-notification-service.text:
• Fully managed pub/sub (publish-subscribe) messaging service
• Publishers send messages to a Topic; all subscribers receive the message (fan-out)
• Supports push delivery — no polling needed by subscribers
• Up to 12.5 million subscriptions per topic, 100,000 topics per account

• Protocols: HTTP/S, Email, SMS, Lambda, SQS, Mobile Push (APNS, GCM, ADM)
• Message Filtering: subscribers receive only messages matching a filter policy
• SNS FIFO Topics: strict ordering + deduplication (only SQS FIFO as subscriber)
• Message Fanout pattern: SNS → multiple SQS queues (decoupled parallel processing)
• SNS + SQS combined: durable, decoupled, fan-out architecture
• Message size: up to 256 KB
• Exam tip: SNS = push, SQS = pull; use both together for resilient fan-out pipelines

amazon-simple-queue-service.text:
• Fully managed message queuing service — oldest AWS service
• Decouple and scale microservices, distributed systems, and serverless apps
• Two queue types: Standard (best-effort ordering, at-least-once) and FIFO (exactly-once, ordered)
• Producers send messages → queue stores them → consumers poll and process

• Default retention: 4 days (max 14 days)
• Max message size: 256 KB (use Extended Client Library for larger payloads via S3)
• Visibility timeout: time a message is hidden after being received (default 30s, max 12h)
• Dead Letter Queue (DLQ): captures messages that fail processing after MaxReceiveCount
• Long Polling: reduces empty responses and API calls (1–20 seconds)
• FIFO throughput: 300 msg/s (3,000 with batching); Standard: unlimited throughput
• Delay Queue: postpone delivery of new messages (0–15 minutes)
• Exam tip: SQS Standard = high throughput; FIFO = ordering & exactly-once

aws-step-functions.text:
• Serverless visual workflow orchestration service
• Coordinates distributed applications and microservices using state machines
• States: Task, Choice, Wait, Parallel, Map, Pass, Succeed, Fail
• Written in Amazon States Language (JSON-based DSL)
• Two workflow types: Standard (exactly-once, up to 1 year) and Express (at-least-once, up to 5 min)

• Integrates with Lambda, DynamoDB, ECS, SNS, SQS, Glue, EMR, Bedrock, and more
• Retry and error handling built in at the state level
• Wait for callback pattern: pause workflow until an external process sends a token back (.waitForTaskToken)
• Activity Tasks: poll-based integration for on-premises or legacy workers
• Express Workflows use case: high-volume, short-duration event processing (IoT, data streaming)
• Standard Workflows use case: long-running business processes, human approval steps
• Exam tip: use Step Functions to orchestrate multi-step serverless workflows instead of chaining Lambdas manually

aws-budgets.text:
• Set custom cost and usage budgets with alerts when thresholds are breached
• Four budget types: Cost, Usage, Savings Plans, Reservation
• Sends alerts via SNS or email before or when forecasted/actual limits are exceeded
• Supports up to 5 SNS notifications per budget
• First two budgets free; $0.02/day per active budget after that

• Budget Actions: automatically apply IAM policies, SCP, or target EC2/RDS instances when a threshold is hit
• Forecasted alerts: warns before you exceed the budget based on projected spend
• Use AWS Organizations to set budgets at account or OU level
• Exam tip: Budgets = proactive spend control; Cost Explorer = historical analysis and forecasting

aws-cost-and-usage-report.part: AWS Cost and Usage Report: Overview
aws-cost-and-usage-report.text:
• Most granular and comprehensive AWS billing report available
• Delivers reports to an S3 bucket in CSV or Parquet format
• Contains line-item details for every AWS service, resource, and tag
• Updated multiple times per day (up to hourly granularity)
• Used for detailed cost allocation, chargeback, and showback

aws-cost-and-usage-report.part: AWS Cost and Usage Report: Key Concepts (SAA-C03)
aws-cost-and-usage-report.text:
• Integrates with Athena (Parquet format), Redshift, and QuickSight for analysis
• Cost allocation tags: activate tags to track costs at resource level in reports
• Supports AWS Organizations: see costs across all member accounts
• Exam tip: CUR = most detailed billing data; use Athena to query CUR in S3 for cost insights

aws-cost-explorer.text:
• Visualize, understand, and manage AWS costs and usage over time
• Provides historical data (up to 12 months) and forecasts (up to 12 months ahead)
• Free to access; some advanced features (hourly granularity, resource-level data) cost extra
• Pre-built and custom reports with filterable dimensions (service, region, tag, account)

• Rightsizing Recommendations: identifies underutilized EC2 instances to downsize
• Savings Plans and Reserved Instance recommendations with estimated savings
• Cost anomaly detection: ML-powered alerts for unexpected cost spikes
• API access: query programmatically ($0.01 per request)
• Exam tip: Cost Explorer = visualization + forecasting + recommendations; Budgets = proactive alerts + actions

amazon-ec2.text:
• Elastic Compute Cloud — virtual servers (instances) in the cloud (IaaS)
• Choose OS (Linux, Windows, macOS), CPU, RAM, storage, and network
• EC2 User Data: bootstrap script run once on first launch as root user
• Security Groups: virtual firewall at the instance level (stateful)
• Key pairs: SSH access (Linux) or RDP decryption (Windows)

amazon-ec2.part: Amazon EC2: Instance Types & Purchasing Options (SAA-C03)
amazon-ec2.text:
• General Purpose (t, m): balance of compute, memory, networking
• Compute Optimized (c): HPC, batch processing, gaming servers
• Memory Optimized (r, x, z): in-memory databases, real-time analytics
• Storage Optimized (i, d, h): high I/O, data warehouses, distributed filesystems
• Purchasing: On-Demand, Reserved (1 or 3 yr, up to 72% savings), Spot (up to 90% savings, can be interrupted), Dedicated Host, Dedicated Instance, Capacity Reservations
• Spot Instances: set a max price; terminated with 2-minute warning when spot price exceeds your bid

• EBS: persistent network-attached block storage (survives stop/start)
• Instance Store: ephemeral physically attached storage — fastest I/O, data lost on stop/terminate
• ENI (Elastic Network Interface): virtual network card; can be moved between instances
• Placement Groups: Cluster (low latency, same AZ), Spread (max 7 per AZ, fault isolation), Partition (distributed, for Hadoop/Kafka/Cassandra)
• Hibernate: saves RAM state to EBS — instance resumes faster; max 60 days

aws-app-runner.text:
• Fully managed service to deploy containerized web apps and APIs at scale
• No infrastructure management — provide source code or container image
• Auto-scales from zero; pay only for compute used
• Built-in load balancing, TLS, CI/CD integration (GitHub or ECR)
• Use case: quickly deploy microservices, APIs, or web frontends without Kubernetes/ECS expertise

aws-batch.text:
• Fully managed batch processing at any scale — dynamically provisions EC2 or Spot Instances
• Runs hundreds of thousands of batch computing jobs
• No need to install and manage batch computing software
• Jobs are defined as Docker container images
• Exam distinction: Batch = any runtime (Docker), multi-node; Lambda = serverless but limited runtime (15 min max)

• Job Queue → Job Definition → Compute Environment
• Managed Compute Environments: AWS manages EC2 or Fargate capacity
• Unmanaged Compute Environments: you manage EC2 instances
• Spot Instances support: cost-optimized batch jobs with interruption handling
• Use case: genomics, financial risk modeling, rendering, ML training jobs
• Exam tip: Batch = long-running compute jobs; Lambda = short-lived event-driven functions

aws-elastic-beanstalk.text:
• PaaS — deploy and manage applications without managing infrastructure
• Supports: Java, .NET, PHP, Node.js, Python, Ruby, Go, Docker
• Automatically handles: load balancing, scaling, monitoring, deployments
• You retain full control of the underlying AWS resources
• Free to use — pay only for the resources it provisions

• Deployment policies: All at once (fastest, downtime), Rolling, Rolling with additional batch, Immutable (safest), Blue/Green
• .ebextensions folder: customize and configure the EB environment using YAML/JSON
• Supported tiers: Web Server (ELB + EC2) and Worker (SQS + EC2 for background tasks)
• Beanstalk with RDS: attach RDS inside the environment (dev) or external (prod — survives env deletion)
• Exam tip: Beanstalk = fastest way to deploy apps; dev/test focus; not for fine-grained infra control

aws-lambda.text:
• Serverless Functions as a Service (FaaS)
• Run code without provisioning or managing servers
• Supported runtimes: Node.js, Python, Java, C#, Go, Ruby, custom runtimes via Lambda Layers
• Max execution timeout: 900 seconds (15 minutes)
• Memory: 128 MB to 10,240 MB (CPU scales proportionally)

• Pay per invocation (first 1M free) + duration (GB-second)
• Concurrency: default 1,000 per region; Reserved Concurrency and Provisioned Concurrency available
• Lambda@Edge / CloudFront Functions: run code at edge locations
• VPC integration: Lambda can access resources in a VPC
• Environment variables: store config; use KMS for encryption
• Layers: share common code/dependencies across functions
• Lambda SnapStart (Java): reduces cold start latency significantly
• Triggers: API Gateway, ALB, S3, DynamoDB Streams, Kinesis, SQS, SNS, EventBridge, Cognito, and more

amazon-elastic-container-registry.text:
• Fully managed Docker container image registry
• Store, manage, and deploy container images privately or publicly
• Public registry: Amazon ECR Public (gallery.ecr.aws)
• Integrated with ECS, EKS, and Fargate for seamless deployments
• Images are encrypted at rest and in transit

• Lifecycle Policies: automate cleanup of old or unused images to reduce storage costs
• Image Scanning: detect software vulnerabilities using Basic or Enhanced (Inspector) scanning
• Cross-region and cross-account replication of images
• Immutable image tags: prevent image tags from being overwritten
• Access control via IAM resource-based policies
• Exam tip: ECR = private Docker registry on AWS; always pair with ECS/EKS for container deployments

amazon-elastic-container-service.text:
• Fully managed container orchestration service
• Run Docker containers on a cluster of EC2 instances (EC2 launch type) or serverlessly (Fargate)
• Task Definition: JSON blueprint specifying container image, CPU, memory, ports, volumes, and IAM role
• Task: a running instance of a Task Definition
• Service: maintains a desired number of running tasks with optional load balancer integration

• EC2 launch type: you manage the underlying EC2 instances; must install ECS Agent
• Fargate launch type: serverless — AWS manages the infrastructure
• IAM Roles: EC2 Instance Profile (EC2 type) vs Task Role (per-task permissions)
• ECS Service Auto Scaling: scale tasks using Target Tracking, Step, or Scheduled policies
• EFS integration: shared persistent storage across tasks and AZs
• Load Balancer integration: ALB (dynamic port mapping), NLB (high throughput)
• Exam tip: assign Task Role (not Instance Profile) for per-task AWS API permissions

amazon-elastic-kubernetes-service.text:
• Fully managed Kubernetes control plane on AWS
• Run Kubernetes workloads without installing/operating your own control plane
• Node types: Managed Node Groups (EC2, auto-provisioned), Self-managed Nodes, Fargate (serverless)
• Supports EC2 and Fargate launch types

• EKS Anywhere: deploy EKS on on-premises infrastructure
• EKS Distro: AWS-distributed Kubernetes version for self-managed clusters
• Pod IAM roles via IRSA (IAM Roles for Service Accounts)
• EKS Add-ons: manage CoreDNS, kube-proxy, VPC CNI, EBS CSI driver
• EKS integrates with ECR, ALB (AWS Load Balancer Controller), CloudWatch Container Insights
• Exam tip: ECS = AWS-native container orchestration; EKS = Kubernetes-compatible; choose EKS for existing K8s workloads

aws-fargate.text:
• Serverless compute engine for containers — works with ECS and EKS
• No EC2 instances to provision, manage, or scale
• Define required CPU and memory per task; AWS handles the rest
• Pay for the vCPU and memory resources requested per second

• Each Fargate task runs in its own isolated kernel and compute environment
• Fargate Spot: run interruptible Fargate workloads at up to 70% discount
• Storage: ephemeral storage up to 200 GB per task (shared between containers in a task)
• For persistent storage: mount EFS volumes
• ECS + Fargate = fully serverless container platform; no infrastructure overhead
• Exam tip: use Fargate when you want containers without managing EC2; use EC2 launch type when you need GPU or specific instance families

amazon-aurora.text:
• AWS-built relational database — fully compatible with MySQL and PostgreSQL
• 5x faster than MySQL, 3x faster than PostgreSQL
• Auto-scales storage from 10 GB to 128 TB in 10 GB increments
• High availability: 6 copies of data across 3 AZs; self-healing storage
• Up to 15 read replicas; up to 5 for MySQL RDS

• Aurora Serverless v2: auto-scales capacity in fine-grained increments per application demand
• Aurora Global Database: 1 primary region + up to 5 read-only secondary regions; RPO < 1s, RTO < 1 min
• Aurora Multi-Master: multiple write nodes for continuous write availability
• Backtrack: rewind the database to any point in time without restoring a snapshot
• Aurora Replicas: auto failover in < 30 seconds; serve read traffic
• Exam tip: Aurora = best performance + HA for MySQL/PostgreSQL on AWS; use Global DB for multi-region DR

amazon-documentdb.text:
• Fully managed document database compatible with MongoDB workloads
• AWS's implementation of MongoDB (not actual MongoDB — uses Aurora storage tech)
• Scales storage automatically up to 64 TB
• 6 copies of data across 3 AZs; continuous backup to S3

• Use case: migrate existing MongoDB workloads to AWS without code changes
• Supports MongoDB 3.6, 4.0 API
• Up to 15 read replicas for read scaling
• Exam tip: DocumentDB = MongoDB-compatible managed NoSQL on AWS; DynamoDB = AWS-native NoSQL (key-value + document)

amazon-dynamodb.text:
• Fully managed, serverless, key-value and document NoSQL database
• Single-digit millisecond performance at any scale
• Automatic scaling; multi-AZ by default; 99.999% SLA with Global Tables
• Tables: items (rows) with attributes (columns); no fixed schema
• Primary Key: Partition Key only (hash), or Partition Key + Sort Key (composite)

• DynamoDB Streams: ordered record of item changes (24h retention) → triggers Lambda
• Global Tables: multi-region, multi-active replication for low-latency global access
• DAX (DynamoDB Accelerator): in-memory cache, microsecond read latency
• TTL: automatically delete items after an expiry timestamp — no extra charge
• Point-in-time Recovery (PITR): restore to any second in the past 35 days
• Capacity modes: Provisioned (with optional Auto Scaling) vs On-Demand
• Transactions: ACID transactions across multiple items and tables
• Exam tip: DynamoDB = serverless NoSQL; use DAX for read-heavy caching; Global Tables for multi-region active-active

amazon-elasticache.text:
• Fully managed in-memory caching service
• Two engines: Redis and Memcached
• Reduces load on databases for read-heavy workloads
• Sub-millisecond latency

• Redis: Multi-AZ with auto-failover, Read Replicas, data persistence, Pub/Sub, Sorted Sets, backup/restore, encryption at rest/in-transit
• Memcached: multi-threaded, no replication, no persistence, no encryption — pure horizontal scaling
• Use Redis for: leaderboards, session stores, real-time analytics, pub/sub, geospatial
• Use Memcached for: simple caching, multi-threaded workloads where persistence is not needed
• Exam tip: if the question mentions HA, failover, or persistence → Redis; pure caching performance → Memcached

amazon-rds.text:
• Fully managed relational database service (OLTP)
• Supported engines: MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server, and Amazon Aurora
• AWS handles: provisioning, OS patching, backups, monitoring, scaling, failover
• Automated backups: daily full backup + transaction logs (PITR up to 35 days)
• Manual snapshots: retained indefinitely

• Multi-AZ: synchronous standby replica for automatic failover (same region) — NOT for read scaling
• Read Replicas: asynchronous read scaling (up to 5); can be cross-region; can be promoted to standalone
• RDS Proxy: connection pooling — reduces DB connections, improves Lambda/ECS resilience; supports IAM auth and Secrets Manager
• Storage Auto Scaling: automatically scales EBS storage when free space is low
• Exam tip: Multi-AZ = HA/DR; Read Replicas = performance/scaling; RDS Proxy = connection pooling for serverless

aws-database-migration-service.text:
• Managed database migration service — migrate databases to AWS quickly and securely
• Source database remains fully operational during migration (minimal downtime)
• Supports homogeneous migrations (MySQL → MySQL) and heterogeneous (Oracle → Aurora)
• For heterogeneous migrations: use AWS Schema Conversion Tool (SCT) first to convert schema

• Runs on a Replication Instance in your VPC
• Continuous Data Replication (CDC): keeps target in sync with source after initial load
• Sources: Oracle, SQL Server, MySQL, MariaDB, PostgreSQL, MongoDB, S3, IBM DB2
• Targets: RDS, Aurora, Redshift, DynamoDB, S3, Elasticsearch, Kafka, DocumentDB
• Multi-AZ replication instance for high availability
• Exam tip: DMS = database migration with minimal downtime; SCT = schema conversion for heterogeneous migrations

aws-command-line-interface.text:
• Command-line tool for interacting with AWS services using commands in your terminal
• Uses access keys (Access Key ID + Secret Access Key) for authentication
• Configured via: aws configure (stores credentials in ~/.aws/credentials)
• Works with IAM users, IAM roles (via STS), and instance profiles
• Version 2 is the current recommended version

• --dry-run flag: check permissions without actually executing the command
• STS decode-authorization-message: decode error messages from unauthorized API calls
• Named profiles: switch between multiple AWS accounts using --profile
• EC2 Instance Metadata: curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ — no IAM needed
• MFA with CLI: use sts get-session-token with MFA token to get temporary credentials
• Exam tip: never store access keys on EC2 — use IAM Instance Roles instead

aws-cloud-development-kit.text:
• Define cloud infrastructure using familiar programming languages: TypeScript, Python, Java, .NET, Go
• CDK synthesizes code into CloudFormation templates
• Higher-level abstractions (Constructs) over raw CloudFormation resources
• CDK Deploy → synthesizes template → CloudFormation stack
• Constructs Library: pre-built components for common patterns

• L1 Constructs (Cfn*): direct CloudFormation resource mapping
• L2 Constructs: higher-level with defaults and helper methods
• L3 Constructs (Patterns): opinionated multi-resource patterns (e.g., ApplicationLoadBalancedFargateService)
• CDK Bootstrapping: deploys a CDKToolkit stack in your account (S3 bucket + IAM roles)
• cdk synth → cdk diff → cdk deploy
• Exam tip: CDK = infrastructure as real code; generates CloudFormation under the hood

aws-cloudshell.text:
• Browser-based shell with pre-authenticated AWS CLI access
• No need to configure credentials — uses your console session's permissions
• Pre-installed: AWS CLI, Python, Node.js, git, and common tools
• 1 GB of persistent storage per region
• Available in most AWS regions
• Exam tip: use CloudShell for quick CLI commands without local setup or access key configuration

aws-codeartifact.text:
• Fully managed artifact repository for software packages
• Supports npm, PyPI, Maven, Gradle, NuGet, generic packages
• Integrates with CodeBuild, CodePipeline, and developer IDEs
• Acts as a proxy to public repositories (npmjs.com, PyPI) and caches packages internally
• Exam tip: CodeArtifact = secure, central package store for builds; prevents fetching from the public internet each time

aws-codebuild.text:
• Fully managed build service — compiles code, runs tests, produces artifacts
• No servers to manage; scales automatically
• Uses buildspec.yml file in the source root to define build commands
• Outputs: artifacts stored in S3; logs in CloudWatch Logs or S3
• Charged per build minute

• Build environments: prebuilt Docker images for common languages or custom images from ECR
• VPC support: run builds inside a VPC to access private resources
• buildspec.yml phases: install → pre_build → build → post_build
• Environment variables: use SSM Parameter Store or Secrets Manager for sensitive values
• Exam tip: CodeBuild = CI build/test phase; no servers; buildspec.yml drives it

aws-codecommit.text:
• Fully managed private Git repository service
• Hosted on AWS — no need to manage your own Git server
• Encrypted at rest (KMS) and in transit
• Authentication: HTTPS (Git credentials or AWS CLI credential helper) or SSH
• IAM controls repository access; resource-based policies supported
• Note: AWS announced end-of-life for CodeCommit new customer creation in 2024 — existing customers continue. Exam may still include it.

aws-codedeploy.text:
• Fully managed deployment service — automates code deployments to EC2, on-premises servers, Lambda, ECS
• Does NOT provision infrastructure — deploys to existing targets
• Works with any programming language and OS
• appspec.yml (or appspec.json): defines deployment lifecycle hooks

• Deployment types: In-place (EC2/on-prem only) and Blue/Green (EC2, Lambda, ECS)
• Deployment configurations: AllAtOnce, HalfAtATime, OneAtATime, custom canary/linear for Lambda/ECS
• Deployment Groups: set of instances or Lambda functions targeted for deployment
• Rollback: automatic on failure or CloudWatch alarm trigger
• CodeDeploy Agent: must be installed on EC2/on-prem instances
• Exam tip: CodeDeploy = deployment automation to existing infrastructure; Blue/Green = zero-downtime deployments

aws-codepipeline.text:
• Fully managed CI/CD pipeline orchestration service
• Automates build, test, and deploy phases of release process
• Pipeline stages: Source → Build → Test → Deploy
• Integrates with CodeCommit, GitHub, S3 (source), CodeBuild (build), CodeDeploy, ECS, Beanstalk, CloudFormation (deploy)

• Artifacts flow between stages via S3 buckets
• Manual Approval action: pause pipeline and require human approval before proceeding
• EventBridge integration: trigger pipelines on events or schedule
• Cross-region actions: deploy to multiple regions from a single pipeline
• Exam tip: CodePipeline = orchestrates the CI/CD workflow; glues together CodeCommit + CodeBuild + CodeDeploy

aws-x-ray.text:
• Distributed tracing service for debugging and analyzing microservices and serverless apps
• Provides end-to-end view of requests as they travel through your application
• Identifies bottlenecks, latency issues, and errors across services
• Service Map: visual representation of application components and their connections

• X-Ray Daemon: sidecar process that collects and sends trace data to X-Ray API
• Traces: composed of Segments (per service) and Subsegments (granular operations)
• Annotations: indexed key-value pairs used to filter traces
• Metadata: non-indexed data for contextual information
• Integration: Lambda, EC2, ECS, Beanstalk, API Gateway, ALB, SNS, SQS, DynamoDB
• Sampling: controls how much data is recorded — default 5% + 1 request/second
• Exam tip: X-Ray = distributed tracing; use for debugging latency in microservices

aws-amplify.text:
• Full-stack toolset for building and hosting web and mobile applications
• Amplify Studio: visual development environment
• Amplify Hosting: CI/CD + CDN hosting for frontend apps (similar to Vercel/Netlify)
• Amplify Libraries: client-side libraries for Auth, API, Storage, Analytics

• Backend: provisions S3, DynamoDB, Cognito, Lambda, API Gateway, AppSync automatically
• Authentication: Cognito User Pools integration built in
• GraphQL API: powered by AppSync
• Hosting supports: SSR (Next.js), SSG, SPA
• Feature branches: deploy separate environments per Git branch
• Exam tip: Amplify = fastest way to build full-stack serverless web/mobile apps; integrates all the AWS pieces automatically

aws-appsync.text:
• Managed GraphQL API service
• Connects to multiple data sources: DynamoDB, Lambda, RDS, HTTP endpoints, OpenSearch
• Real-time subscriptions: push data updates to clients via WebSockets
• Offline sync: Amplify DataStore uses AppSync for conflict resolution and offline data

• Resolvers: connect GraphQL operations to data sources (VTL or Lambda)
• Direct Lambda Resolver: call Lambda directly without VTL templates
• Caching: server-side caching per resolver or full request caching
• Authorization modes: API Key, AWS IAM, Cognito User Pools, OIDC, Lambda
• Exam tip: AppSync = managed GraphQL; preferred over REST when clients need real-time subscriptions or flexible data fetching

amazon-comprehend.text:
• Natural Language Processing (NLP) service using ML
• Extracts insights from unstructured text: entities, key phrases, language, sentiment, PII
• Fully managed and serverless
• Comprehend Medical: detects medical entities and PHI in clinical text
• Use case: analyze customer feedback, classify support tickets, redact PII from documents

amazon-kendra.text:
• Intelligent enterprise search service powered by ML
• Indexes documents from S3, SharePoint, Salesforce, databases, OneDrive, and more
• Returns precise answers, not just document links (question-answering)
• Incremental learning from user interactions improves results over time
• Exam tip: Kendra = intelligent document search; Comprehend = text analysis/NLP; don't confuse them

amazon-lex.text:
• Build conversational chatbots and voice interfaces using the same technology as Alexa
• Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR): convert speech to text
• Natural Language Understanding (NLU): recognize intent
• Integrates with Lambda for fulfillment logic
• Deployed to web, mobile, Slack, Twilio, Facebook Messenger
• Exam tip: Lex = chatbot/voice bot builder; Polly = text-to-speech; Transcribe = speech-to-text

amazon-polly.text:
• Turns text into lifelike speech using deep learning
• Standard voices and Neural voices (NTTS) for more natural-sounding output
• SSML (Speech Synthesis Markup Language): control speech style, pitch, rate
• Lexicons: customize pronunciation of specific words
• Speech Marks: return metadata about where words/sentences appear in the audio stream
• Use case: audiobooks, accessibility, voice assistants, notifications

amazon-rekognition.text:
• ML-powered image and video analysis service
• Capabilities: object/scene detection, facial analysis, facial recognition, text detection, content moderation, celebrity recognition, custom labels
• No ML expertise required
• Rekognition Video: analyze stored video (S3) or streaming video (Kinesis Video Streams)
• Use case: identity verification, unsafe content moderation, media asset management

amazon-sagemaker-ai.text:
• Fully managed ML platform for building, training, and deploying ML models
• Removes the heavy lifting from each step of the ML process
• Integrated Jupyter Notebooks: explore and visualize data
• SageMaker Studio: unified ML IDE

• Built-in Algorithms: XGBoost, linear learner, k-means, image classification, etc.
• SageMaker Autopilot: AutoML — automatically builds and tunes best model
• SageMaker Pipelines: CI/CD for ML workflows
• SageMaker Feature Store: store, share, and manage ML features
• Batch Transform: run inference on large datasets without persistent endpoints
• SageMaker Clarify: detect bias in datasets and model predictions
• Exam tip: SageMaker = end-to-end ML platform; questions often test which component to use (training vs. inference vs. feature engineering)

amazon-textract.text:
• ML service to extract text and structured data from scanned documents automatically
• Goes beyond OCR — extracts forms (key-value pairs) and tables
• Supports PDFs and images (JPEG, PNG, TIFF)
• Synchronous (small docs) and asynchronous (large/multi-page docs) APIs
• Use case: automate document processing — invoices, tax forms, medical records, contracts
• Exam tip: Textract = document data extraction; Rekognition = image/video analysis

amazon-transcribe.text:
• Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) — convert audio to text
• Features: speaker identification (diarization), custom vocabulary, custom language models, PII redaction
• Transcribe Medical: clinical audio transcription (physician dictation)
• Streaming transcription: real-time low-latency transcription via WebSockets
• Use case: meeting transcription, call center analytics, subtitle generation, medical documentation

amazon-translate.text:
• Neural machine translation service
• Translates text between 75+ languages
• Supports real-time and batch translation
• Custom Terminology: ensure brand names, technical terms are translated correctly
• Use case: localize websites, translate user-generated content, multilingual chatbots
• Exam tip: Translate = language translation; Comprehend = language detection + NLP analysis

amazon-cloudwatch.text:
• Monitoring and observability service for AWS resources and applications
• Collects metrics, logs, and events
• Default EC2 metrics: CPU, Network, Disk I/O (every 5 min); detailed monitoring = 1 min
• Custom Metrics: push your own metrics using PutMetricData API (RAM, disk space, etc.)

• CloudWatch Alarms: trigger actions (SNS, EC2 action, Auto Scaling) based on metric thresholds
• CloudWatch Logs: collect, store, and analyze log files from EC2, Lambda, CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs
  - Log Groups → Log Streams
  - Log Insights: interactive query language for log analysis
  - Subscriptions: stream logs to Lambda, Kinesis Data Streams, or Firehose
• CloudWatch Dashboards: global, cross-account metric visualization
• CloudWatch Events / EventBridge: react to AWS service events
• CloudWatch Container Insights: monitoring for ECS, EKS, Kubernetes on EC2
• CloudWatch Contributor Insights: identify top contributors to metrics (top IPs, users, etc.)

aws-cloudformation.text:
• Infrastructure as Code (IaC) — define AWS resources using YAML or JSON templates
• Provisions and manages stacks of related AWS resources
• Free service — pay only for provisioned resources
• Repeatable, version-controlled infrastructure deployments

• Template sections: AWSTemplateFormatVersion, Description, Parameters, Mappings, Conditions, Resources (required), Outputs
• Stack: a collection of AWS resources managed as a single unit
• StackSets: deploy stacks across multiple accounts and regions
• Change Sets: preview changes before executing an update
• Drift Detection: identify manual configuration changes to stack resources
• Nested Stacks: reuse template components across stacks
• DeletionPolicy: Retain, Snapshot (databases), Delete (default)
• Exam tip: CloudFormation = IaC for AWS; CDK generates CloudFormation; Beanstalk uses CloudFormation under the hood

aws-cloudtrail.text:
• Records API calls and console actions across your AWS account
• Enabled by default — 90-day event history in the console
• Trail: send logs to S3 (indefinite retention) and optionally CloudWatch Logs
• Applies to all regions or a single region
• Three types of events: Management Events (control plane), Data Events (S3 object ops, Lambda invocations), Insights Events (unusual activity)

• CloudTrail Insights: detect unusual write API activity (e.g., spike in EC2 RunInstances)
• Log file integrity validation: SHA-256 hashing + digital signing to detect tampering
• Organization Trails: single trail covering all accounts in AWS Organizations
• Deliver to S3 + query with Athena for compliance and audit analysis
• Exam tip: CloudTrail = 'who did what and when'; CloudWatch = 'what is happening now'; Config = 'what changed'

aws-config.text:
• Assess, audit, and evaluate the configurations of AWS resources
• Records configuration history and changes over time
• Evaluate resources against desired configurations using Config Rules
• Per-region service but can aggregate across regions and accounts
• Stores configuration snapshots and history in S3

• Config Rules: AWS managed rules (150+) or custom Lambda-backed rules
• Compliance: COMPLIANT or NON_COMPLIANT per resource per rule
• Remediation: auto-remediate non-compliant resources using SSM Automation Documents
• Configuration Recorder: must be enabled to record resource configurations
• Conformance Packs: bundle of Config rules + remediation actions as a single template
• Exam tip: Config = configuration compliance tracking; CloudTrail = API audit; use Config when asked 'has this resource always been configured this way?'

aws-health-dashboard.text:
• Provides personalized visibility into AWS service health events affecting your account
• Service Health Dashboard (status.aws.amazon.com): global status of all AWS services
• Personal Health Dashboard (PHD): events and scheduled changes that may impact YOUR resources
• Proactive notifications via EventBridge for automated remediation
• Exam tip: PHD = alerts about AWS events impacting your specific resources; use EventBridge rules to automate responses to health events

aws-license-manager.text:
• Centrally manage software licenses from vendors (Microsoft, SAP, Oracle, IBM) across AWS and on-premises
• Set license rules to prevent license violations
• Integrates with AWS Organizations for cross-account visibility
• Tracks license usage against entitlements
• Exam tip: License Manager = governance for BYOL software licenses; avoids over- or under-provisioning costly licenses

aws-management-console.text:
• Web-based GUI to access and manage AWS services
• Protected by username + password + optional MFA
• Supports cross-account role switching in the console
• Resource Groups: group AWS resources by tags and manage them together
• Tag Editor: add/edit tags across multiple resources and regions
• Exam tip: Console = easiest but least automatable access; use CLI or SDK for scripts/automation

aws-organizations.text:
• Manage multiple AWS accounts centrally
• Hierarchical structure: Root → Organizational Units (OUs) → Accounts
• Consolidated Billing: single payment method, volume discounts, shared Reserved Instances and Savings Plans
• Management Account: master payer; cannot restrict with SCPs

• Service Control Policies (SCPs): restrict permissions for member accounts and OUs (applied in addition to IAM; deny overrides)
• SCPs do NOT apply to the management account
• Tag Policies: enforce consistent tagging across accounts
• Delegated Administrator: assign a member account to manage specific services (e.g., Security Hub, GuardDuty)
• AWS RAM (Resource Access Manager): share resources across accounts in the Organization
• Exam tip: SCPs = account-level guardrails; cannot grant permissions, only restrict; permissions are intersection of IAM + SCP

aws-systems-manager.text:
• Operational management platform for EC2, on-premises servers, and VMs
• No extra cost for most features; SSM Agent must be installed on instances (pre-installed on Amazon Linux 2 / Windows AMIs)
• Hybrid support: manage on-premises servers as managed instances

• Session Manager: secure shell/RDP access without opening port 22/3389 or using bastion hosts
• Run Command: execute scripts or commands across many instances without SSH
• Patch Manager: automate OS and application patching
• Parameter Store: secure storage for configuration and secrets (plain text or SecureString via KMS)
• Automation: define and run runbooks (SSM Documents) for common operational tasks
• Inventory: collect metadata about instances (OS, apps, network config)
• Exam tip: Session Manager = preferred secure shell access (no key pairs, no open ports); Parameter Store = lightweight secret/config storage

aws-trusted-advisor.text:
• Automated best-practice recommendations across 5 pillars
• Categories: Cost Optimization, Performance, Security, Fault Tolerance, Service Limits
• Basic/Developer support: 7 core security + service limit checks
• Business/Enterprise support: full check access + API access
• Weekly email notifications for check summaries
• Exam tip: Trusted Advisor = proactive best-practice checks; use with Organizations for org-wide visibility

aws-well-architected-tool.text:
• Free tool to review your architectures against the 6 pillars of the Well-Architected Framework
• 6 Pillars: Operational Excellence, Security, Reliability, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, Sustainability
• Generates a report with high/medium risk findings and improvement recommendations
• Workload: unit of review — a collection of AWS resources forming an application
• Custom Lenses: define your own best-practice questions
• Exam tip: know the 6 pillars and what each covers — common in SAA-C03 scenario questions

amazon-kinesis-video-streams.text:
• Securely stream video from connected devices to AWS for analytics, ML, playback, and processing
• Automatically provisions and elastically scales infrastructure to ingest video from millions of devices
• Stores video durably in S3 and encrypts in transit/at rest
• Producers: cameras, smartphones, drones, audio feeds, RADAR, LIDAR
• Consumers: Rekognition Video, SageMaker, custom ML apps, Amazon Kinesis Data Streams
• Use case: smart home cameras, industrial monitoring, autonomous vehicles, video ML pipelines

aws-application-discovery-service.text:
• Discover on-premises servers and dependencies to plan cloud migrations
• Two discovery modes:
  - Agentless (VMware): uses Agentless Collector appliance — collects VM inventory and performance data without installing agents
  - Agent-based: install Discovery Agent on each server — collects detailed performance, network, and process data
• Data stored in AWS Migration Hub
• Integrates with AWS Migration Evaluator for business case
• Exam tip: use ADS as the first step of a migration to map your environment before choosing a migration strategy

aws-application-migration-service.text:
• Lift-and-shift (rehost) migration service — successor to CloudEndure Migration
• Continuously replicates source servers (physical, virtual, cloud) to AWS using block-level replication
• Minimal cutover window: testing happens in staging while production remains live
• Supports Windows and Linux
• Replicated to EC2 instances in target region
• Exam tip: MGN = lift-and-shift with minimal downtime; ADS = discovery before migration

aws-datasync.text:
• Fully managed data transfer service — move large amounts of data to/from AWS
• Automated, scheduled, incremental data transfers
• Sources: on-premises NAS/SAN (NFS, SMB), S3-compatible storage, HDFS; also AWS to AWS transfers
• Destinations: S3, EFS, FSx for Windows, FSx for Lustre, FSx for OpenZFS
• Uses DataSync Agent (VM appliance) for on-premises connections

• Encrypts data in transit using TLS; integrity validation end-to-end
• Transfer speed: up to 10 Gbps per agent
• Scheduling: one-time or recurring transfers
• Preserves metadata: file permissions, timestamps
• DataSync vs. Storage Gateway: DataSync = bulk data migration/sync; Storage Gateway = ongoing hybrid access
• DataSync vs. Snowball: DataSync = online transfer (network); Snowball = offline transfer (physical device)
• Exam tip: DataSync = automated fast file migration; Storage Gateway = ongoing hybrid integration

aws-transfer-family.text:
• Fully managed file transfer service into/out of S3 and EFS using standard protocols
• Supported protocols: SFTP, FTPS, FTP, AS2
• No need to change existing file transfer workflows or clients
• Managed endpoints with custom hostnames (Route 53)
• Authentication: service-managed, custom Lambda, Active Directory, Okta, Azure AD
• Use case: migrate legacy SFTP-based file exchange to S3 without changing client software

amazon-api-gateway.text:
• Fully managed service to create, publish, maintain, monitor, and secure REST, HTTP, and WebSocket APIs
• Acts as the 'front door' for applications to access backend services
• Integrates with Lambda, HTTP endpoints, AWS services, VPC links
• Handles: traffic management, CORS, authorization, throttling, API versioning, caching

• API types: REST API (feature-rich), HTTP API (lower cost, lower latency), WebSocket API (real-time two-way)
• Deployment types: Edge-Optimized (CloudFront), Regional, Private (VPC)
• Caching: TTL 300s default (0–3600s); reduces backend calls
• Throttling: 10,000 RPS default, 5,000 burst limit per account
• Usage Plans + API Keys: control access and rate limits per client
• Canary Deployments: send a % of traffic to a new stage for testing
• Exam tip: API Gateway + Lambda = core serverless API pattern; HTTP API is cheaper for simple proxy use cases

amazon-cloudfront.text:
• CDN (Content Delivery Network) — cache and deliver content from 400+ edge locations globally
• Reduces latency for static and dynamic content
• DDoS protection via AWS Shield Standard (always on)
• Origins: S3 bucket, ALB, EC2, API Gateway, custom HTTP origins

• Origin Access Control (OAC): restrict S3 bucket access to CloudFront only
• Cache Behaviors: route requests by path pattern to different origins or with different cache rules
• Cache invalidation: invalidate objects before TTL expires ($0.005 per path after first 1,000)
• Geo Restriction: allowlist or blocklist countries
• CloudFront Functions: lightweight JS functions at edge (< 1ms); for request/response manipulation
• Lambda@Edge: more powerful Node.js/Python functions at edge for complex logic
• Field-level encryption: encrypt sensitive data at the edge
• Signed URLs / Signed Cookies: restrict access to premium content
• Exam tip: CloudFront = CDN/caching + global reach; Global Accelerator = TCP/UDP routing optimization, not caching

amazon-route-53.text:
• Highly available, scalable cloud DNS web service
• Domain registration, DNS routing, and health checking
• 100% SLA uptime guarantee
• Hosted Zones: public (internet-facing) or private (VPC internal)
• Supports all DNS record types: A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, NS, SOA, TXT, CAA, DS, NAPTR, PTR, SOA, SPF, SRV

• Simple: single resource; no health checks
• Weighted: distribute traffic by percentage across resources
• Latency-based: route to the region with lowest network latency
• Failover: primary + secondary (active-passive); requires health check
• Geolocation: route based on user's geographic location
• Geoproximity: route based on geographic location with bias adjustments (requires Route 53 Traffic Flow)
• Multi-value: up to 8 healthy records returned (NOT a substitute for ELB)
• IP-based: route based on client IP CIDR ranges
• Health Checks: monitor endpoints, CloudWatch alarms, or other health checks (calculated)

elastic-load-balancing.text:
• Distributes incoming traffic across multiple targets (EC2, containers, Lambda, IP addresses)
• Three types: Application Load Balancer (ALB), Network Load Balancer (NLB), Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB)
• Managed service — AWS handles scaling and high availability
• Integrates with EC2 Auto Scaling, ECS, ACM (SSL/TLS), CloudWatch

• ALB (Layer 7): HTTP/HTTPS/gRPC; content-based routing (path, host, headers, query string); target groups: EC2, ECS, Lambda, IP; ideal for microservices
• NLB (Layer 4): TCP/UDP/TLS; extreme performance (millions RPS, ultra-low latency); static IP per AZ; preserves client IP; ideal for gaming, IoT, financial apps
• GWLB (Layer 3): route traffic to virtual appliances (firewalls, IDS/IPS); uses GENEVE protocol on port 6081
• Sticky Sessions: route same client to same target using cookies (ALB or CLB)
• Cross-zone load balancing: distribute traffic evenly across all AZs (enabled by default on ALB)
• Connection Draining (Deregistration Delay): allow in-flight requests to complete before deregistering a target (1–3600s)

aws-direct-connect.text:
• Dedicated private network connection from your data center to AWS
• Bypasses the public internet — more consistent latency and bandwidth
• Connection speeds: 1 Gbps, 10 Gbps, 100 Gbps (via AWS Direct Connect locations)
• Sub-1Gbps via hosted connections through AWS partners
• Takes over 1 month to establish a new Direct Connect connection

• Virtual Interfaces (VIFs): Private VIF (access VPC), Public VIF (access AWS public services), Transit VIF (access via Transit Gateway)
• Direct Connect Gateway: connect to multiple VPCs across regions using a single connection
• Encryption: Direct Connect is NOT encrypted by default; use VPN over Direct Connect for encryption in transit
• Resiliency: use two connections for HA; connection + VPN as backup
• Exam tip: Direct Connect = dedicated private connectivity; lower latency, higher bandwidth; use VPN as backup or for encryption

aws-global-accelerator.text:
• Routes traffic over the AWS global network backbone to improve availability and performance
• Provides two static Anycast IP addresses as entry points from anywhere in the world
• Traffic enters the AWS network at the nearest edge location and travels over the private backbone to the endpoint
• Endpoints: ALB, NLB, EC2, Elastic IPs in any region

• Health checks: automatically routes traffic around unhealthy endpoints
• Traffic Dials: control % of traffic directed to endpoint groups per region
• Weighted Endpoints: control traffic distribution within an endpoint group
• No caching — routes actual requests (vs. CloudFront which caches content)
• Client IP preservation: available for ALB and EC2 endpoints
• Exam tip: Global Accelerator = lower latency + failover for non-HTTP (TCP/UDP) and HTTP workloads; CloudFront = caching + CDN for HTTP content delivery

aws-transit-gateway.text:
• Network hub to connect multiple VPCs, VPNs, and Direct Connect connections centrally
• Eliminates complex VPC peering meshes (hub-and-spoke model)
• Transitive routing: traffic can flow between any connected network through the TGW
• Regional resource — use TGW peering for cross-region connectivity

• Attachments: VPC, VPN, Direct Connect Gateway, TGW peering, SD-WAN appliances
• Route Tables: control which attachments can communicate with each other (isolation or shared)
• Equal Cost Multipath (ECMP): increase bandwidth by load-balancing across multiple VPN tunnels
• Multicast support: distribute one-to-many traffic across connected VPCs
• Exam tip: Transit Gateway = simplify large network topologies; replaces complex VPC peering; supports 5,000 attachments per TGW

amazon-cognito.text:
• User identity and access management for web and mobile applications
• Two components: User Pools and Identity Pools (Federated Identities)
• User Pools: user directory for sign-up/sign-in; returns JWT tokens
• Identity Pools: exchange tokens (from Cognito, social providers, SAML) for temporary AWS credentials via STS

• User Pools features: MFA, password policies, email/phone verification, built-in UI, social identity federation (Google, Facebook, Apple)
• Identity Pools: grant authenticated or unauthenticated users direct AWS resource access
• Cognito Sync (now replaced by AppSync): sync user profile data across devices
• JWT token types: ID Token (user info), Access Token (API access), Refresh Token
• Exam tip: User Pools = authentication (who are you?); Identity Pools = authorization to AWS services (what can you access?); often used together

amazon-detective.text:
• Analyzes, investigates, and identifies the root cause of security findings
• Automatically collects data from GuardDuty, CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, EKS audit logs
• Uses ML, statistical analysis, and graph theory to build an interactive graph
• No configuration needed — fully managed
• Exam tip: Detective = deep security investigation tool; GuardDuty = threat detection; Security Hub = aggregated findings; Detective is the 'why and how' of a GuardDuty finding

amazon-guardduty.text:
• Intelligent threat detection service using ML and anomaly detection
• Analyzes: CloudTrail Events, VPC Flow Logs, DNS Logs, EKS Audit Logs, RDS Login Events, S3 Data Events
• No agents, no additional software — enable with one click
• Findings: types like UnauthorizedAccess, CryptoCurrency, Backdoor, Trojan, etc.
• EventBridge: trigger automated responses to findings (Lambda, SNS)

• Multi-account support via AWS Organizations
• Trusted IP lists and threat intel feeds: customize threat detection
• GuardDuty Malware Protection: scan EBS volumes attached to potentially compromised EC2 instances
• Finding suppression rules: filter out known-benign findings
• 30-day free trial
• Exam tip: GuardDuty = automated threat detection; does NOT block traffic itself — use WAF, Security Groups, or Lambda for automated remediation

amazon-inspector.text:
• Automated vulnerability assessment for EC2 instances, container images in ECR, and Lambda functions
• Continuously scans workloads for software vulnerabilities (CVEs) and unintended network exposure
• For EC2: SSM Agent required; scans OS packages and network reachability
• For ECR: scans on push or on schedule
• Findings sent to Security Hub and EventBridge
• Exam tip: Inspector = vulnerability scanning; GuardDuty = threat/anomaly detection; Macie = S3 data classification

amazon-macie.text:
• ML-powered security service to discover and protect sensitive data in Amazon S3
• Automatically identifies and alerts on PII, financial data, credentials, and other sensitive data types
• Generates findings routed to Security Hub or EventBridge
• Multi-account support via Organizations
• Exam tip: Macie = S3 data privacy and PII detection; Inspector = vulnerability scanning; GuardDuty = threat detection

amazon-verified-permissions.text:
• Fine-grained authorization service using Cedar policy language
• Centralize and externalize authorization logic from application code
• Works with Cognito User Pools for authentication context
• Define policies for resources and actions; application calls Verified Permissions to authorize requests
• Exam tip: Verified Permissions = application-level fine-grained authz (RBAC/ABAC); IAM = AWS resource access control

aws-artifact.text:
• Self-service portal for on-demand access to AWS compliance reports and security documentation
• Reports: SOC 1/2/3, PCI DSS, ISO 27001, FedRAMP, HIPAA BAA, GDPR DPA, and more
• Artifact Agreements: review and accept DPAs, BAAs, and NDAs
• Free service
• Exam tip: Artifact = compliance documentation/reports; not a security monitoring tool

aws-audit-manager.text:
• Continuously audit AWS usage to simplify risk assessment and compliance with regulations
• Automates evidence collection from AWS services (CloudTrail, Config, Security Hub, etc.)
• Pre-built frameworks: PCI DSS, GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2, CIS benchmarks
• Custom frameworks and controls
• Generates audit-ready reports for assessors
• Exam tip: Audit Manager = automated evidence collection for audits; Artifact = access to AWS's own compliance reports

aws-certificate-manager.text:
• Provision, manage, and deploy SSL/TLS certificates
• Free public certificates for use with AWS services
• Auto-renewal of managed certificates before expiry
• Supported services: ALB, CloudFront, API Gateway, Elastic Beanstalk, AppSync (via API GW)
• Cannot export certificates managed by ACM (private key stays in ACM)
• Private CA: ACM Private CA issues private certificates for internal PKI
• Exam tip: ACM = free managed SSL/TLS certs for AWS services; import external certs if needed; certificates are regional (except CloudFront which requires us-east-1)

aws-directory-service.text:
• Managed Microsoft Active Directory or simple AD in the cloud
• Three options:
  - AWS Managed Microsoft AD: full Windows AD in AWS VPC; supports MFA, trust relationships with on-prem AD
  - AD Connector: proxy that redirects AD requests to on-premises AD — no AD data stored in AWS
  - Simple AD: standalone managed directory powered by Samba 4 (subset of AD features; no trust support)
• Exam tip: Managed Microsoft AD = full AD in cloud; AD Connector = use existing on-prem AD; Simple AD = small/low-cost AD without on-prem

aws-identity-and-access-management.text:
• Global service for managing access to AWS services and resources
• Root account: full access; should NOT be used for daily tasks
• IAM Users: individual identities; assigned policies
• IAM Groups: logical grouping of users; policies attached to group apply to all members
• IAM Roles: temporary identity assumed by services, users, or external identities

• Policy types: Identity-based (user/group/role), Resource-based (S3, KMS, Lambda), Permissions Boundary, SCP, Session Policy
• Policy evaluation: explicit Deny > explicit Allow > implicit Deny
• Least privilege principle: grant only the minimum permissions required
• Access Analyzer: identify resources shared with external entities
• IAM Credentials Report: list all users and credential status
• STS AssumeRole: get temporary credentials to assume a role across accounts
• Permission Boundaries: set the maximum permissions an entity can have
• Exam tip: policies are evaluated — Deny always wins; use Conditions for MFA enforcement, IP restriction, tag-based access

aws-iam-identity-center.text:
• Centralized SSO and user management for AWS accounts and cloud applications
• Successor to AWS Single Sign-On (SSO)
• One login to access multiple AWS accounts and business applications (Salesforce, GitHub, Office 365)
• Integrates with AWS Organizations for multi-account access management
• Identity sources: built-in identity store, Active Directory (AWS Managed AD or AD Connector), external IdPs (Okta, Azure AD, PingFederate) via SAML 2.0

aws-iam-identity-center.part: AWS IAM Identity Center: Key Concepts (SAA-C03)
aws-iam-identity-center.text:
• Permission Sets: define the access a user or group has in an AWS account (maps to an IAM role)
• Attribute-based Access Control (ABAC): use user attributes from IdP to control access
• Free service — included with AWS Organizations
• Exam tip: IAM Identity Center = centralized SSO for many AWS accounts; ideal for organizations using multiple accounts; replaces manual cross-account role federation

aws-key-management-service.text:
• Managed service for creating and controlling encryption keys
• Integrated with 100+ AWS services (S3, EBS, RDS, DynamoDB, Lambda, etc.)
• Keys never leave KMS unencrypted
• FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated HSMs underpin the service (CloudHSM = Level 3 dedicated)

• CMK types: AWS Managed Keys (auto-rotated yearly, free), Customer Managed Keys (you control policy, $1/month), AWS Owned Keys (multi-tenant, no visibility)
• Symmetric (AES-256): single key for encrypt + decrypt — most common
• Asymmetric (RSA, ECC): public/private key pair — for signing or encryption outside AWS
• Key Policies: resource-based policy required to control access to KMS keys
• Envelope Encryption: KMS encrypts a data key; data key encrypts data — used for large data
• KMS Multi-Region Keys: same key material in multiple regions for cross-region encrypt/decrypt
• Exam tip: KMS = encryption key management; use Envelope Encryption for files >4KB; Key Policies control access to keys

aws-network-firewall.text:
• Managed stateful network firewall and intrusion detection/prevention (IDS/IPS) service for VPCs
• Deployed in a dedicated subnet in each AZ
• Supports: stateless packet filtering, stateful rules, deep packet inspection, domain/URL filtering
• Rule types: Stateless (5-tuple), Stateful (Suricata-compatible), Domain lists (FQDN filtering)
• Centralize across accounts and VPCs using AWS Firewall Manager
• Exam tip: Network Firewall = Layer 3-7 protection within VPC; WAF = Layer 7 HTTP protection for ALB/CloudFront/API GW; Security Groups = instance-level stateful firewall

aws-resource-access-manager.text:
• Share AWS resources with any AWS account or within AWS Organizations
• Avoids duplicating resources in multiple accounts
• Shareable resources: Subnets (VPC sharing), Transit Gateway, Route 53 Resolver Rules, License Manager configs, Aurora DB clusters, ACM Private CA, Glue catalogs, and more
• Cross-account sharing without VPC peering
• Exam tip: RAM = share resources across accounts; VPC Subnet sharing = allow multiple accounts to launch resources into the same VPC

aws-secrets-manager.text:
• Manage, retrieve, and rotate secrets (DB credentials, API keys, tokens) securely
• Automatic rotation: integrates with RDS, Aurora, Redshift, DocumentDB — rotates credentials automatically using Lambda
• Encrypted with KMS
• Charged per secret per month + per 10,000 API calls

• Cross-account access: share secrets via resource-based policies
• Replication: replicate secrets to multiple regions for DR
• Secrets Manager vs SSM Parameter Store: Secrets Manager = auto-rotation + designed for secrets; SSM Parameter Store = free tier, config + secrets, no auto-rotation
• Exam tip: when question mentions 'automatic rotation of database credentials' → Secrets Manager is the answer

aws-security-hub.text:
• Aggregates security findings from multiple AWS services into a single dashboard
• Sources: GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, IAM Access Analyzer, Firewall Manager, Config, Partner solutions
• Evaluates against CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark and other security standards automatically
• Sends aggregated findings to EventBridge for automation
• Multi-account support via Organizations
• Exam tip: Security Hub = single pane of glass for security findings; does not detect threats itself — aggregates from other services

aws-shield.text:
• DDoS protection service
• Shield Standard: free, automatic, always-on protection against most common Layer 3/4 DDoS attacks
• Shield Advanced: paid ($3,000/month per org), 24/7 DDoS response team (DRT), financial protection against scaling cost during attacks, advanced attack diagnostics
• Shield Advanced covers: EC2, ELB, CloudFront, Route 53, Global Accelerator
• Exam tip: Shield Standard = free automatic DDoS protection included with AWS; Shield Advanced = enterprise-grade DDoS protection with SRT access and cost protection

aws-waf.text:
• Web Application Firewall — filter HTTP/HTTPS traffic at Layer 7
• Deployed on: ALB, API Gateway, CloudFront, AppSync, Cognito User Pool, App Runner
• Web ACL: collection of rules evaluated in priority order
• Rule types: IP sets, SQL injection, XSS, size constraints, geo-match, rate-based, regex match

• Managed Rule Groups: pre-built rules from AWS or AWS Marketplace partners (OWASP Top 10, etc.)
• Rate-based Rules: automatically block IPs that exceed a request threshold (DDoS mitigation)
• Bot Control: detect and manage bot traffic
• Web ACL Capacity Units (WCUs): limit on rule complexity per Web ACL
• AWS Firewall Manager: centrally manage WAF rules across accounts and resources
• Exam tip: WAF = HTTP-level protection (SQLi, XSS, geo block, rate limit); Shield = volumetric DDoS; Network Firewall = VPC-level deep packet inspection

amazon-elastic-block-store.text:
• Network-attached persistent block storage for EC2 instances
• Tied to a specific AZ — must be in same AZ as the EC2 instance
• Can detach and reattach to another instance in the same AZ
• Multi-Attach: attach one io1/io2 volume to multiple EC2 instances in the same AZ (cluster applications)
• Billed per GB provisioned per month

• gp2/gp3 (General Purpose SSD): most use cases; gp3 independently set IOPS and throughput
• io1/io2 (Provisioned IOPS SSD): high-performance DBs; io2 Block Express = up to 256,000 IOPS
• st1 (Throughput Optimized HDD): big data, data warehouses, log processing; cannot be boot volume
• sc1 (Cold HDD): infrequently accessed, lowest cost; cannot be boot volume
• EBS Snapshots: incremental backups to S3; can copy across regions; can create AMIs; Snapshot Archive (75% cheaper, 24–72h restore)
• EBS Encryption: transparent encryption using KMS; snapshots of encrypted volumes are encrypted
• Exam tip: know which volume type to use by IOPS/throughput requirements; only SSD types can be boot volumes

amazon-elastic-file-system.text:
• Fully managed, scalable NFS (Network File System) file storage
• Multi-AZ access: mount from multiple EC2 instances across AZs simultaneously
• Elastic: automatically grows and shrinks as you add/remove files — no capacity planning
• Only available for Linux (POSIX-compliant)

• Performance modes: General Purpose (default), Max I/O (higher latency, higher throughput)
• Throughput modes: Bursting (scales with storage), Provisioned (fixed), Elastic (auto-scales, recommended for spiky workloads)
• Storage tiers: Standard, Standard-IA (Infrequent Access), Archive — use Lifecycle Policies to move files automatically
• EFS Access Points: enforce user identity and root directory per application
• Encryption at rest (KMS) and in transit (TLS)
• EFS vs EBS: EFS = shared multi-instance NFS; EBS = single-instance block storage (except io1/io2 Multi-Attach)

amazon-s3.text:
• Infinitely scalable object storage; objects stored in buckets; buckets are region-specific
• Max object size: 5 TB; use multipart upload for objects > 5 GB (recommended > 100 MB)
• Globally unique bucket names; accessed via URL or API
• Strong read-after-write consistency (since Dec 2020)

• S3 Standard: 99.99% availability, 11 9s durability, frequent access
• S3 Intelligent-Tiering: auto-moves objects between tiers based on access patterns; no retrieval fees
• S3 Standard-IA: infrequent access; lower storage cost but per-retrieval fee; min 30-day storage
• S3 One Zone-IA: single AZ, infrequent access; 20% cheaper than Standard-IA; min 30 days
• S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval: millisecond retrieval; min 90-day storage
• S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval: Expedited (1-5 min), Standard (3-5h), Bulk (5-12h); min 90 days
• S3 Glacier Deep Archive: cheapest; Standard (12h), Bulk (48h); min 180 days

• Versioning: keep multiple versions of objects; once enabled, can only be suspended (not disabled)
• Replication: Cross-Region (CRR) or Same-Region (SRR); requires versioning on both buckets
• Lifecycle Rules: transition and expire objects automatically
• S3 Event Notifications: trigger Lambda, SNS, SQS, or EventBridge on object operations
• Transfer Acceleration: upload to edge location → private AWS network to S3
• Object Lock (WORM): prevent deletion/overwrite for a fixed period (Governance or Compliance mode)
• S3 Select / Glacier Select: retrieve subset of data using SQL — reduces data transferred
• S3 Access Logs: log all requests to a separate S3 bucket
• Presigned URLs: grant temporary access to private objects
• Exam tip: know storage class use cases, lifecycle policies, replication prerequisites (versioning), and encryption options (SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, SSE-C, client-side)

amazon-s3-glacier.text:
• Ultra-low-cost storage for archival and long-term backup
• Three retrieval options for Glacier Flexible Retrieval: Expedited (1-5 min), Standard (3-5 hours), Bulk (5-12 hours)
• Glacier Deep Archive: cheapest storage on AWS; Standard retrieval 12h, Bulk 48h
• Vault Lock: enforce compliance controls (WORM) on a vault; cannot be changed once locked
• Minimum storage duration: 90 days (Glacier), 180 days (Deep Archive)
• Exam tip: Glacier = cost-optimized archival; Vault Lock = WORM compliance; use Lifecycle Rules to auto-transition from S3 to Glacier

aws-backup.text:
• Centrally manage and automate data protection across AWS services
• Supported services: EC2, EBS, RDS, Aurora, DynamoDB, EFS, FSx, S3, Storage Gateway, DocumentDB, Neptune, VMware on AWS
• Backup Plans: define schedules, retention periods, and lifecycle rules
• Backup Vault: encrypted storage for backups; apply resource-based policies
• Backup Vault Lock: WORM mode — even admins cannot delete backups (compliance requirement)

• Cross-Region backups: copy backups to another region for DR
• Cross-Account backups: copy backups to another AWS account via Organizations
• On-demand and scheduled backups
• Tag-based backup: automatically back up resources based on tags
• AWS Backup Audit Manager: audit and report on backup compliance
• Exam tip: AWS Backup = centralized backup across all supported services; use when asked for a single backup strategy across multiple AWS services

aws-storage-gateway.text:
• Hybrid cloud storage service bridging on-premises environments with AWS storage
• Available as a hardware appliance or VM image
• Three gateway types: S3 File Gateway, FSx File Gateway, Volume Gateway, Tape Gateway

• S3 File Gateway: NFS/SMB access to S3; files stored as objects in S3; local cache for low-latency access
• FSx File Gateway: native SMB/NFS access to FSx for Windows File Server with local cache
• Volume Gateway – Stored: primary data on-premises, async backup to S3 as EBS snapshots
• Volume Gateway – Cached: primary data in S3, frequently accessed data cached on-premises
• Tape Gateway: virtual tape library (VTL) backed by S3 and Glacier — replace physical tape infrastructure
• Exam tip: Storage Gateway = extend on-prem storage to S3; DataSync = migrate/transfer data; Storage Gateway is ongoing hybrid integration
